Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

David Rosenthal

b. 1939

Higher-order theories of consciousness: what makes a mental state conscious.

David M. Rosenthal’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed around one decisive thesis: consciousness is not the essence of mentality; it is a higher-order way in which mental reality appears to a subject. The mind, on his view, contains beliefs, desires, perceptions, sensations, emotions, and sensory qualities whether or not those states are conscious. Consciousness is not an inner light intrinsic to those states. It is a further representational relation: a mental state becomes conscious when the subject is suitably aware of being in that state, and Rosenthal’s distinctive claim is that this awareness normally consists in a higher-order thought.

This makes his work one of the most systematic late twentieth- and early twenty-first-century attempts to preserve a robust, realist psychology while rejecting the Cartesian assumption that the mental is transparent to consciousness. His official research profile lists philosophy of mind, consciousness, intentionality, sensation, the self, psychology, and neuroscience among his central areas, which accurately reflects the range of his system.

1. Rosenthal’s central project

Rosenthal’s work is not only a theory of consciousness. It is a broader philosophy of psychology with several connected parts:

First, a theory of state consciousness: what makes a particular mental state conscious rather than unconscious.

Second, a theory of mental qualities: what sensory qualities such as color-experiences, pains, sounds, tastes, and bodily feelings are, independently of whether they are conscious.

Third, a theory of self-awareness and introspection: how we come to be aware of our own mental states, and why such awareness is fallible.

Fourth, a theory of mental appearance and mental reality: why what our mental life seems like from the first-person point of view may diverge from the actual structure of our mental life.

Fifth, a methodological thesis: philosophy of mind should not begin by treating first-person phenomenology as metaphysically decisive. It should distinguish what consciousness reveals from what mental states really are.

His 2005/2006 book Consciousness and Mind is the main systematic collection. Its abstract summarizes the central doctrine: a sensation, thought, or other mental state is conscious when one has a higher-order thought that one is in that state. The same summary notes that the book also develops a theory of mental qualities that individuates them independently of consciousness and uses this to address qualitative consciousness, speech, self-interpretation, and the unity of consciousness.

Rosenthal’s project is therefore best read as a naturalistic anti-Cartesian philosophy of mind. It rejects the identification of mind with consciousness, but it does not collapse the mind into behavior, neural activation, or mere computational function. Mental states are real psychological states. Consciousness is one feature some of them acquire.

2. The historical problem: consciousness after unconscious mentality

The historical background matters deeply. In much ancient and early modern philosophy, mental states were often treated as automatically conscious. Rosenthal repeatedly emphasizes that traditional writers typically spoke not of mental states “being conscious” but of our being conscious of our mental states. Descartes, Locke, and their contemporaries did not usually use “conscious” as a one-place predicate of mental states; the modern phrase “a conscious state” becomes theoretically important only once philosophers and psychologists take seriously the existence of mental states of which one is wholly unaware.

This point gives Rosenthal his historical orientation. The central modern problem is not simply “What is consciousness?” but rather: given that some mental states are conscious and others are not, what explains the difference?

That problem becomes acute only after the post-Cartesian acceptance of unconscious mentality. Rosenthal’s historical narrative moves through Aristotle, Descartes, Locke, Brentano, and Freud. In his encyclopedia discussion, he notes that Descartes held that we cannot have a thought of which we are not aware at the moment it is in us, while Brentano still maintained in 1874 that all mental states are conscious, even though Brentano opened the door to the intelligibility of unconscious mental states.

Freud is especially important as a historical pivot. Freud made unconscious mentality unavoidable, but Rosenthal thinks the Freudian tradition did not go far enough, because Freud still resisted genuinely unconscious feelings or qualitative states. Rosenthal’s more radical anti-Cartesian move is to say that even sensory qualitative character can occur unconsciously. In a recent article, Rosenthal explicitly observes that Freud held “the mental” to be in itself unconscious while nevertheless denying that feelings can occur unconsciously.

Rosenthal’s historical position is therefore between three traditions:

He rejects Cartesian transparency, because not all mental states are conscious.

He rejects behaviorist elimination or deflation, because unconscious mental states are real psychological states.

He rejects phenomenalist or intrinsicist theories of qualia, because qualitative character is not essentially conscious.

This makes him one of the central figures in the higher-order, naturalistic, representationalist wing of contemporary philosophy of mind.

3. The three senses of consciousness

Rosenthal’s entire system depends on a strict taxonomy. Much confusion in consciousness studies, he argues, comes from treating several different phenomena as one. He distinguishes at least three.

Creature consciousness is the condition of an organism when it is awake, responsive, and not asleep, anesthetized, or comatose. This is consciousness as a state of the whole creature.

Transitive consciousness is being conscious of something: seeing a tree, hearing a bell, feeling a surface, or thinking of something as present.

State consciousness is the property of a mental state itself: a perception, belief, desire, pain, or emotion is conscious rather than unconscious.

This distinction is explicit in Rosenthal’s work and in standard discussions of higher-order theories. His own “Concepts and Definitions of Consciousness” distinguishes being awake, being conscious of something, and the consciousness of mental states, and stresses that conflating them causes theoretical difficulty. The Stanford Encyclopedia likewise treats the distinction between creature consciousness and mental-state consciousness as one of the advances of recent philosophy of consciousness, noting that higher-order theories focus mainly on state consciousness.

The key Rosenthalian question is therefore not: “What makes a person awake?” Nor is it simply: “What is it to perceive an object?” The key question is:

What makes a mental state one that the subject is conscious of being in?

That is the problem of state consciousness.

4. The transitivity principle

Rosenthal’s first major thesis is the transitivity principle:

A mental state is conscious only if the subject is, in some suitable way, conscious of being in that state.

The principle is called “transitivity” because it explains the one-place property “M is conscious” in terms of the two-place relation “S is conscious of M.” Rosenthal argues that no mental state of which one is in no way aware intuitively counts as conscious. The Oxford Handbook chapter states the point directly: state consciousness is explained in terms of transitive consciousness, and higher-order theories differ over how the subject is conscious of the state.

This is not supposed to be circular. The awareness that makes a state conscious need not itself be conscious. A thought, perception, or representation can make one aware of something without itself being a conscious mental state. Rosenthal uses this to avoid an infinite regress: a first-order state is conscious because of a higher-order awareness, but that higher-order awareness need not itself be conscious unless a further higher-order state represents it.

This is one of Rosenthal’s most important departures from Cartesian and phenomenological traditions. Consciousness is not self-luminous. It is not the automatic self-presence of mental life. It is a relation generated by further mental representation.

5. Higher-order thought theory

Rosenthal’s distinctive version of higher-order theory is the higher-order thought theory, usually abbreviated HOT theory.

The basic claim is:

A mental state M is conscious when the subject has a suitable higher-order thought to the effect that they are in M.

The higher-order thought represents the subject as being in the first-order mental state. For example, a pain becomes conscious when one has a higher-order thought representing oneself as being in pain. A visual experience of red becomes conscious when one has a higher-order thought representing oneself as having a visual state of that qualitative kind.

This is not usually a deliberate judgment. It is not typically introspection. The higher-order thought is normally unconscious, spontaneous, and not reached by conscious inference. Rosenthal’s “Consciousness and Metacognition” argues that although our awareness of conscious states seems immediate and unmediated, this appearance can be explained by unconscious higher-order thoughts that occur without conscious inference or conscious mediation.

Rosenthal rejects inner-sense theories, which explain consciousness by positing a quasi-perceptual inner faculty that senses one’s own mental states. The inner-sense model initially looks attractive because awareness of our own conscious states seems immediate and spontaneous. But Rosenthal argues that there is no independent evidence for a special inner sense organ or modality, and that such a model has difficulty explaining why only some mental states are sensed while others remain unconscious.

The HOT model also explains why consciousness is connected to reportability. If a state is conscious because one is aware of being in it, then one is normally in a position to report it. Reporting a state is asserting, verbally or otherwise, that one is in it. Rosenthal therefore treats reportability not as the essence of consciousness, but as a predictable consequence of higher-order awareness.

6. The architecture of the mind

A Rosenthalian philosophy of mind has a layered psychological architecture.

At the first level are first-order mental states: perceptions, sensations, beliefs, desires, emotions, intentions, and memories. These states have their own causal, representational, and functional properties. They may be conscious or unconscious.

At the second level are higher-order thoughts or higher-order awarenesses. These represent the subject as being in first-order states. When suitable, they make those first-order states conscious.

At the third level are introspective or reflective states. If one becomes conscious of the higher-order thought itself, that requires a further higher-order representation. This is why ordinary consciousness is not the same as introspection.

This structure avoids the classic regress objection. A conscious state does not require a conscious higher-order state; it requires only a higher-order state. If that higher-order state is itself conscious, that is a further phenomenon.

Lau and Rosenthal’s 2011 review puts the point in terms of conscious awareness depending on higher-order mental representations that represent oneself as being in particular mental states. It also notes that higher-order states are typically not conscious themselves.

This layered architecture is one of the reasons Rosenthal’s theory is attractive to philosophers interested in psychology. It separates ordinary mental processing, conscious awareness, introspection, and metacognition rather than treating them as one phenomenon.

7. Consciousness as mental appearance

Rosenthal’s mature formulation is that consciousness is mental appearance. Consciousness is the way mental life subjectively appears to the subject. But appearance is not reality. A subject’s mental life can appear one way while the underlying first-order mental reality is different.

This is crucial. It means Rosenthal rejects Cartesian infallibility about one’s own conscious states. We may be wrong about our own mental states because higher-order awareness can misrepresent them.

In “Higher-order awareness, misrepresentation and function,” Rosenthal argues that conscious mental states are states we are aware of, but the higher-order awareness can misdescribe the first-order state. He explicitly compares this to metacognition, which can misrepresent one’s cognitive states and abilities.

This is not an accidental feature of the theory. It is essential. If consciousness is a representational appearance, then it can be more or less accurate. If higher-order awareness were guaranteed to match mental reality, the theory would collapse back toward a Cartesian transparency model.

The upshot is a powerful distinction:

Mental reality: the actual first-order psychological states and qualities one is in.

Mental appearance: how those states are represented to the subject through higher-order awareness.

The same distinction is central to Rosenthal’s recent work on mental qualities. He argues that rejecting the appearance/reality distinction in consciousness is effectively an unsupported insistence that consciousness is intrinsic to qualitative character.

8. Mental qualities and qualitative consciousness

Rosenthal’s theory of consciousness is inseparable from his theory of mental qualities.

Many philosophers treat qualitative character as intrinsically conscious. On that view, a pain, a red experience, or an auditory tone cannot exist unconsciously, because its qualitative character is precisely what it is like for the subject.

Rosenthal rejects that. He argues that qualitative character and consciousness are distinct. A sensory state can have a qualitative character without being conscious. Conscious qualitative experience is not a primitive fusion of quality and awareness; it is a sensory quality plus a higher-order awareness of oneself as being in a state with that quality.

His recent article “Qualities and Consciousness” states the thesis sharply: the view that qualitative character is intrinsically conscious lacks serious support, creates mystery, and is contradicted by empirical evidence for unconscious qualitative character. Rosenthal instead offers an account of mental qualities based on perceptual discrimination, not consciousness.

This is one of his most important contributions to philosophy of mind. Many higher-order theories are theories of consciousness only. Rosenthal’s theory also tries to explain what sensory qualities are independently of consciousness.

9. Quality-space theory

Rosenthal’s positive theory of mental qualities is a quality-space theory, earlier associated in his work with a homomorphism theory of mental qualities.

The idea is that sensory qualities are individuated by their position in a structured space of discriminable differences. The relevant data are perceptual discriminations, especially just noticeable differences. For color, the quality space reflects dimensions such as hue, saturation, and brightness. For spatial vision, it reflects size, shape, and location.

A mental quality is not an isolated intrinsic glow. It is fixed by its relative location among other qualities in a discriminative structure. Red-like qualities differ from orange-like qualities, blue-like qualities, and purple-like qualities in a structured way. The quality is what it is because of its place in that network of perceptual discrimination.

Rosenthal’s theory therefore treats conscious perceptual discrimination as relational. Mental qualities are fixed comparatively, and the theory appeals to discriminative ability rather than to consciousness.

This allows Rosenthal to say that mental qualities can occur unconsciously. If a sensory state plays the right discriminatory role, it has a mental quality even if the subject is not conscious of it. Consciousness is not needed to individuate the quality.

10. Unconscious qualitative character

The doctrine of unconscious qualitative character is perhaps Rosenthal’s most controversial thesis. It is also one of the most important for extracting a comprehensive philosophy of mind from his work.

He argues that empirical findings support unconscious perception with qualitative properties. In “Qualities and Consciousness,” he cites masked color stimuli that subjects report not seeing but nevertheless discriminate above chance, blindsight cases involving stimuli with qualitative properties, and color-constancy findings for unseen surfaces.

The philosophical significance is large. If qualitative character can occur unconsciously, then the “hard problem” is not the problem of explaining why physical or functional states have intrinsic conscious feel. It becomes two separate problems:

What are sensory qualities?

What makes some sensory qualities conscious?

Rosenthal answers the first with quality-space theory and the second with higher-order thought theory.

This two-factor strategy is the core of his philosophy of mind. It rejects the assumption that qualitative character and consciousness are inseparable.

11. Conscious qualitative experience

For Rosenthal, a conscious visual experience of red involves two components.

First, there is a first-order visual state with a mental quality fixed by its role in perceptual discrimination.

Second, there is a suitable higher-order thought representing the subject as being in a state with that quality.

The “what-it-is-like” character of the experience is not an intrinsic luminous property of the first-order quality. It is the way the subject is aware of being in a state with that quality. Rosenthal says in recent work that there being something it is like for one is the mental appearance of being in a state with that qualitative character; a higher-order awareness makes one aware of oneself as being in a qualitative state of that kind.

This yields a distinctive answer to the problem of phenomenal consciousness:

Phenomenal consciousness is not denied.

Qualitative character is not denied.

First-person appearance is not denied.

What is denied is that these are primitive, intrinsic, unanalyzable features of first-order mental states.

12. The hard problem, Mary, and inverted spectrum

Rosenthal’s response to the hard problem is diagnostic. He thinks the hard problem arises because philosophers build consciousness into qualitative character and then find themselves unable to explain qualitative character in nonconscious, objective terms.

If mental qualities are individuated only by how they consciously appear, then they become mysterious. They seem detached from objective psychological, perceptual, and causal relations. This generates worries about explanatory gaps, Mary, inverted spectra, and ineffable qualia.

Rosenthal’s reply is to deny the starting assumption. Mental qualities are not individuated by conscious appearance. They are individuated by quality-space structure and perceptual role. Consciousness then adds a higher-order appearance of those qualities.

In “Qualities and Consciousness,” he argues that intrinsicalism about qualitative character creates the sense of an explanatory gap and makes mental qualities seem mysterious. By contrast, if consciousness is not built into qualitative states, one can explain what it is for a state to be conscious by appeal to psychological factors that are not themselves conscious.

This is not a Chalmers-style solution to the hard problem. It is a rejection of the conceptual setup that makes the problem look insoluble.

13. Intentionality and representation

Rosenthal’s philosophy of mind is also representationalist, though not in a simple first-order sense.

Thoughts have intentional content. They represent objects, properties, states of affairs, possibilities, and relations. Sensory qualities also represent, but differently from concepts. A mental quality represents a corresponding stimulus property by occupying a place in a quality space that corresponds to the discriminable structure of stimulus properties. Rosenthal explicitly says mental qualities represent corresponding stimulus properties, but in a way different from conceptual content.

Thus perception has at least two representational dimensions:

A qualitative, sensory, nonconceptual or quasi-nonconceptual dimension.

A conceptual or object-directed dimension, by which one sees something as a tomato, a face, a word, a threat, or a tool.

Consciousness is not required for either kind of representation. A state can represent the world unconsciously. What consciousness adds is not world-directed intentionality but higher-order self-directed representation: the subject is represented as being in a state of a certain kind.

This gives Rosenthal a layered theory of intentionality:

First-order intentionality: the mind represents the world.

Qualitative representation: sensory states represent discriminable stimulus properties.

Higher-order intentionality: the mind represents itself as being in first-order states.

Introspection: the mind consciously represents its own mental states.

14. Thought, speech, and expression

Rosenthal has long been interested in the relation between thought and speech. The connection matters because verbally expressed thoughts are normally conscious. If one sincerely says, “I think the meeting is tomorrow,” the expressed thought is typically conscious.

But Rosenthal does not infer that language creates consciousness. Rather, he explains the connection through higher-order thought. Speech often expresses not only first-order content but a subject’s awareness of being in a state with that content. His work includes essays such as “Why Are Verbally Expressed Thoughts Conscious?” and “Expressing One’s Mind,” and his book’s summary notes that later essays explain the connections among consciousness, speech, self-interpretation, and unity.

This is part of a larger Sellarsian theme in Rosenthal: the concepts we use to understand thoughts and mental states are not simply given by inner awareness. They are shaped by public language, interpretation, and psychological theory. His 2025 article “Thought, Consciousness, and the Given” continues this line by asking how we come to understand the nature of thoughts and acquire the conceptual resources needed to formulate such understanding.

The result is an anti-Cartesian and anti-“Given” account of self-understanding. We do not simply look inward and find fully articulated mental facts. We interpret, classify, express, and theorize mental life.

15. The self and unity of consciousness

Rosenthal’s theory implies a distinctive view of the self.

A conscious state involves a higher-order representation of oneself as being in that state. The self enters consciousness not as a Cartesian ego directly encountered in inner awareness, but as part of the content of higher-order thought.

This gives him a way to explain the unity of consciousness. The unity of consciousness is the sense that one’s conscious states belong to a single subject. On a HOT theory, this unity is not produced by a metaphysical soul or a mental theater. It is produced by higher-order representation: conscious states are represented as states of the same subject.

His CV lists “Unity of Consciousness and the Self,” “Being Conscious of Ourselves,” “Awareness and Identification of Self,” and related essays as part of his sustained work on this topic.

The self, then, is not eliminated. But it is not the foundation of consciousness. It is represented within the higher-order structure that makes states conscious.

16. Introspection and self-knowledge

Rosenthal’s theory sharply distinguishes ordinary consciousness from introspection.

A state can be conscious without being introspected. Ordinary conscious pain, seeing, thinking, or desiring requires only a suitable higher-order thought. Introspection requires conscious attention to one’s own mental state, which means that the higher-order awareness itself becomes conscious or is embedded in further reflective awareness.

This distinction explains why self-knowledge is fallible. If consciousness is mental appearance, and appearance can misrepresent, then first-person access is not incorrigible. One may be wrong about what one feels, wants, thinks, or perceives. This is not an occasional defect; it follows from the representational structure of consciousness.

Rosenthal’s “Higher-order awareness, misrepresentation and function” insists that verbal reports indicate what states an individual is aware of, but not what states occur without the individual’s awareness. It also argues that there is no good reason to treat higher-order awareness as intrinsic to the first-order state.

Self-knowledge is therefore not a direct apprehension of mental reality. It is theory-laden, concept-dependent, and mediated by higher-order representation.

17. Metacognition and consciousness

Rosenthal’s relation to metacognition is subtle.

Metacognition is cognition about cognition: judgments of learning, feelings of knowing, confidence estimates, error monitoring, and evaluations of one’s own cognitive performance. Rosenthal thinks this is relevant to consciousness because both metacognition and consciousness involve higher-order mental states. But he denies that they are the same phenomenon.

In “Consciousness and Metacognition,” he discusses feeling-of-knowing judgments, judgments of learning, confidence, blindsight, visual agnosia, and amnesia as phenomena that make metacognition relevant to consciousness. But he also notes that much metacognition concerns information that is not currently conscious.

In the 2012 article, he makes the distinction sharper: higher-order awarenesses that make states conscious are seldom themselves conscious, whereas metacognitive judgments often are conscious and typically regulate learning, memory, and behavior.

So Rosenthal’s view is not simply “consciousness is metacognition.” Rather:

Ordinary state consciousness is constituted by suitable higher-order awareness.

Metacognition is a broader family of higher-order cognitive capacities.

The two overlap structurally but differ functionally and phenomenologically.

This matters because many contemporary cognitive scientists identify consciousness with confidence, report, monitoring, or metacognitive access. Rosenthal resists that simplification.

18. Neuroscience and empirical psychology

Rosenthal’s theory is philosophical, but it is designed to be empirically constrained.

Lau and Rosenthal’s 2011 review argues that higher-order theories are testable and have empirical support. They contrast higher-order theories with first-order, global workspace, and recurrent-processing theories, and they focus especially on cases where awareness changes while first-order task performance is matched.

The review also links higher-order representation to prefrontal and parietal mechanisms, while warning that not all prefrontal activity is relevant. It distinguishes higher-order theory from global workspace theory: both may appeal to prefrontal-parietal activity, but global workspace theory associates consciousness with broad availability and cognitive control, whereas higher-order theory does not make consciousness itself depend on enhanced task performance.

This is important. Rosenthal does not equate consciousness with neural activation. He thinks a neural theory needs a psychological account of what consciousness is before it can identify neural correlates. His 2012 article says a theory must at minimum explain how conscious mental states differ from nonconscious ones; merely citing neural processes is not enough without an accurate psychological description.

The methodology is therefore neither armchair phenomenology nor reductive neuroscience. It is a theory-mediated philosophy of psychology.

19. Consciousness and function

One of Rosenthal’s most striking claims is that state consciousness may have little or no independent utility.

Creature consciousness obviously has survival value. Being awake and responsive matters. Transitive consciousness of objects also matters. But whether a particular mental state’s being conscious adds functional value beyond the first-order state itself is a different question.

In “Consciousness and Its Function,” Rosenthal argues that while an organism’s being conscious is clearly useful, it is much less clear that a psychological state’s occurring consciously adds utility beyond its occurring unconsciously. He examines proposals involving rational thought, planning, intentional action, executive function, and correction of reasoning, and finds little reason to think state consciousness adds such utility.

This separates him strongly from global workspace approaches, which often define consciousness by its functional role in broadcasting information for reasoning, planning, and control. Rosenthal’s position is more deflationary about the function of state consciousness. The first-order state does the perceptual, cognitive, motivational, or affective work; consciousness is the subject’s higher-order awareness of that state.

This does not mean consciousness is useless in every sense. Consciousness enables report, self-interpretation, and certain forms of communication. But those may be downstream or byproduct functions rather than the essence of consciousness.

20. Misrepresentation and the “false HOT” problem

The false-HOT problem is one of the main objections to Rosenthal.

Suppose a higher-order thought represents me as being in pain, but there is no corresponding first-order pain state. Am I consciously in pain? Does it merely seem to me that I am in pain? Is there a difference?

Rosenthal’s framework pushes toward the view that consciousness is determined by mental appearance: if it subjectively seems to me that I am in pain, then there is something it is like for me as if I am in pain. But this appearance may misrepresent mental reality.

Many critics find this counterintuitive. They think a higher-order representation cannot create a conscious experience unless there is an appropriate first-order state. Rosenthal replies, in effect, that consciousness just is the suitable appearance generated by higher-order awareness. The first-order mental state may be needed for normal cases, causal explanation, and psychological grounding, but consciousness itself is constituted by how the state is represented to the subject.

The same issue appears in Lau and Rosenthal’s discussion of mismatches between first-order and higher-order content: if the first-order state represents green but the higher-order state represents one as seeing red, what determines conscious qualitative character? They note that some higher-order theorists, including Rosenthal, hold that qualitative character is determined by how the higher-order representation makes one aware of one’s first-order state.

This is one of the deepest pressure points in his system.

21. Animals, infants, and nonhuman consciousness

A common objection to HOT theory is that it seems too intellectually demanding. If consciousness requires higher-order thoughts, do infants, nonhuman animals, or cognitively limited creatures lack consciousness?

Rosenthal’s answer is that higher-order thoughts need not be conscious, linguistically explicit, or reflectively self-conscious. They need not involve saying to oneself, “I am now in a visual state.” They can be nonconscious, automatic, and comparatively primitive.

Lau and Rosenthal explicitly note that conscious self-referential thinking requires third-order representation, and that animals or small children may lack that while still having lower-level higher-order representations relevant to awareness. They also point to sensory metacognition in animals such as rats and dolphins as relevant evidence.

Rosenthal’s 2025 paper on Dennett and nonhuman animals is especially relevant. There he defends Dennett’s methodological injunction that we should use our best theory of consciousness to assess animal consciousness, but rejects Dennett’s stronger conclusion that nonhuman animals likely lack conscious mental states. Rosenthal argues that HOT theory provides better leverage for assessing which kinds of mental states in nonhuman animals are likely conscious.

So Rosenthal’s theory is not automatically anti-animal-consciousness. It is anti-sentimental and theory-driven: whether a creature is conscious depends on whether it has the relevant psychological architecture.

22. Rosenthal and the larger development of philosophy of mind

Rosenthal’s historical role can be located through several contrasts.

Against Descartes, Rosenthal denies that mind and consciousness coincide. Descartes treats thought as essentially conscious; Rosenthal treats conscious thought as only one form of thought.

Against Brentano, he accepts the importance of awareness of one’s own mental states but rejects Brentano’s thesis that all mental states are conscious.

Against Freud, he accepts unconscious mentality but extends it more radically, including unconscious qualitative character.

Against behaviorism, he insists on real internal mental states, including unconscious states and sensory qualities.

Against identity theory and early materialism, he is sympathetic to naturalism but argues that neural identity claims must be guided by fine-grained psychological theory.

Against Armstrong and Lycan-style inner-sense theories, he accepts higher-order awareness but rejects the perceptual model of inner monitoring.

Against first-order representationalism, he argues that first-order representational content does not explain why some states are conscious and others are not.

Against Block’s phenomenal/access distinction, he resists treating phenomenal consciousness as an unanalyzed primitive and argues that many debates build in theoretical assumptions about consciousness.

Against Chalmers-style hard-problem dualism, he denies that qualitative consciousness forces us beyond naturalistic psychology; the hard problem is generated by a mistaken intrinsicist conception of qualitative character.

Against Dennett, he shares anti-Cartesian and anti-qualia sympathies but remains more realist about mental qualities and more committed to a determinate higher-order architecture.

Against global workspace theory, he denies that global availability or cognitive broadcasting is what state consciousness consists in.

Against metacognitive reductionism, he distinguishes consciousness from confidence, judgments of learning, and explicit cognitive monitoring.

This places Rosenthal among the central architects of the higher-order tradition. The Stanford Encyclopedia describes higher-order theories as explaining conscious mental states by appeal to a higher-order representation of the state, either a higher-order perception or a higher-order thought. Rosenthal’s distinctive contribution is the actualist, thought-based, extrinsic version of that family, combined with a separate theory of mental qualities.

23. The comprehensive Rosenthalian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive philosophy of mind extracted from Rosenthal would have the following theses.

1. The mental is wider than the conscious.
Mental states can occur unconsciously. This includes not only beliefs and desires but also sensory qualitative states.

2. Consciousness is state-relative, not merely creature-relative.
The central philosophical problem is not just why organisms are awake, but why particular mental states are conscious.

3. State consciousness is higher-order appearance.
A mental state is conscious when the subject is suitably aware of being in it, and this awareness is normally a higher-order thought.

4. Higher-order awareness need not be conscious.
This avoids regress and explains why ordinary consciousness is not introspection.

5. Consciousness is extrinsic to the first-order state.
A state’s being conscious is not an intrinsic property of that state. It depends on a further representational relation.

6. Mental appearance can diverge from mental reality.
First-person access is not infallible. Consciousness can misrepresent.

7. Qualitative character is not intrinsically conscious.
Mental qualities are real, but their nature is fixed by perceptual-discriminative role, not by subjective awareness.

8. Quality-space structure individuates sensory qualities.
Mental qualities are located in spaces of discriminable differences, such as hue-saturation-brightness for color.

9. Conscious qualitative experience is two-factor.
It consists of a first-order qualitative state plus a higher-order awareness representing oneself as being in such a state.

10. Reportability follows from higher-order awareness.
Conscious states are normally reportable because being conscious of a state puts one in a position to assert that one is in it.

11. Introspection is not ordinary consciousness.
Introspection requires further higher-order awareness of one’s mental states or of one’s higher-order awarenesses.

12. The self is represented, not primitively given.
Unity and self-consciousness are explained through higher-order representation of states as belonging to one subject.

13. Consciousness may have little independent function.
The utility of mental states usually derives from their first-order content and causal role, not from their being conscious.

14. Philosophy of mind must be empirically responsible but not empirically naïve.
Neuroscience matters, but it must be guided by psychological distinctions between kinds of consciousness.

15. First-person phenomenology is data, not metaphysics.
How things seem is important, but it does not settle what mental states are.

This is the systematic Rosenthalian picture: the mind is a stratified representational system in which first-order mental reality is made conscious by higher-order mental appearance.

24. Strengths of Rosenthal’s system

Rosenthal’s theory has several major strengths.

It explains the conscious/unconscious contrast directly. Since many mental-state types can occur both consciously and unconsciously, a theory must explain the difference. Rosenthal does so by appealing to higher-order awareness.

It avoids regress. The higher-order state need not itself be conscious.

It preserves mental realism. Unlike eliminativist or behaviorist approaches, it accepts real beliefs, desires, sensations, pains, and qualities.

It avoids primitive qualia. Unlike theories that take phenomenal consciousness as unanalyzable, it gives an account of qualitative character in terms of quality-space structure.

It explains reportability. Conscious states are reportable because the subject is aware of being in them.

It makes self-knowledge fallible. This fits psychoanalysis, social psychology, confabulation, implicit bias, blindsight, and ordinary self-deception better than Cartesian transparency.

It integrates philosophy with empirical psychology. Rosenthal’s work engages subliminal perception, blindsight, metacognition, confidence, prefrontal mechanisms, and report paradigms.

It offers a principled alternative to the hard problem. Rather than positing a metaphysical explanatory gap, it challenges the assumption that qualitative character is intrinsically conscious.

25. Main objections and unresolved problems

The first major objection concerns the transitivity principle. Some philosophers deny that a state must be represented by another state in order to be conscious. First-order theorists argue that the right kind of first-order perceptual content, availability, or recurrent processing may suffice.

The second is the false-HOT problem. If a higher-order thought can occur without a matching first-order state, Rosenthal seems committed to conscious appearances without corresponding mental realities. Some find this acceptable; others think it makes consciousness too detached from sensory psychology.

The third is the division of phenomenal labor. If first-order qualities and higher-order representations diverge, which determines what the experience is like? Rosenthal tends to privilege higher-order appearance, but this remains controversial.

The fourth is animal and infant consciousness. Rosenthal can allow nonlinguistic higher-order representation, but critics worry that the theory still imposes too much cognitive sophistication.

The fifth is empirical. Higher-order theories often appeal to prefrontal mechanisms, but the role of prefrontal cortex in consciousness remains debated. Lau and Rosenthal themselves describe the evidence as supportive but not conclusive, and list outstanding questions about prefrontal regions, qualitative character, development, and nonhuman consciousness.

The sixth is the function problem. If state consciousness adds little independent utility, why is it so widespread? Rosenthal can answer that it is a byproduct of useful capacities such as language, self-monitoring, and interpretation, but this remains an explanatory burden.

The seventh concerns quality-space theory. Quality spaces work most clearly for color and sensory continua, but pain, emotion, affective tone, bodily feeling, and complex multimodal experience are harder to model.

The eighth concerns mental concepts. HOTs represent one as being in a mental state of a certain kind. But how does a subject acquire the relevant concepts of mental states? Rosenthal’s anti-Given and Sellarsian work addresses this, but it is one of the deepest questions for the theory.

26. Final assessment

Rosenthal’s philosophy of mind is best understood as an attempt to complete the anti-Cartesian revolution without losing the reality of the mental.

Freud and cognitive psychology showed that mentality outruns consciousness. Behaviorism and Dennett warned against the myth of a private Cartesian theater. Representationalism and functionalism showed how mental states can be naturalistically individuated. Higher-order theory explains how mental states become conscious. Quality-space theory explains how sensory qualities can be real without being intrinsically conscious.

Rosenthal’s distinctive synthesis is this:

Mental states are real psychological states. Some are qualitative. Some are intentional. Some are conscious. But consciousness is not what makes them mental, intentional, or qualitative. Consciousness is the higher-order way in which some of them appear to the subject.

That is the comprehensive philosophy of mind one can extract from his work. It is realist about mind, naturalist about consciousness, fallibilist about self-knowledge, anti-intrinsicist about qualia, and deeply shaped by the historical transition from Cartesian consciousness to modern unconscious psychology.

PreviousNed Block NextDavid Chalmers

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
